title: The God-Forgotten Election
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ PAT Mן¿½DURMER brought the tidings to the town of God-Forgotten :
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½There are lively days before ye, commin Parlymintן¿½s dissolved!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the boys were all excited, for the State, of course, was ן¿½rotten,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And, in subsequent elections, God-Forgotten was involved.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was little there to live for save in drinking beer and eating;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But we rose on this occasion ere the news appeared in print,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the boys of God-Forgotten, at a wild, uproarious meeting,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nominated Billy Blazes for the commin Parlymint.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Other towns had other favourites, but the day before the battle
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bushmen flocked to God-Forgotten, and the distant sheds were still;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sheep were left to go to glory, and neglected mobs of cattle
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Went a-straying down the river at their sweet bucolic will.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ William Spouter stood for Freetrade (and his votes were split by Nottin),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He had influence behind him and he also had the tin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But across the lonely flatlands came the cry of God-Forgotten,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Vote for Blazes and Protection, and the land youן¿½re living in!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pat Mן¿½Durmer said, ן¿½Ye schaymers, please to shut yer ugly faces,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Lend yer dirty ears a momint while I give ye all a hint:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Keep ye sober till to-morrow and record yer vote for Blazes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½If ye want to send a ringer to the commin Parlymint.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As a young and growinן¿½ township God-Forgottenן¿½s been neglected,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And, if weן¿½d be ripresinted, nowן¿½s the moment to begin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Have the local towns encouraged, local industries purtected:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Vote for Blazes, and Protection, and the land yeן¿½re livinן¿½ in.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I donן¿½t say that William Blazes is a perfect out-anן¿½ outer,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I donן¿½t say he have the larninן¿½, for he never had the luck;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I donן¿½t say he have the logic, or the gift of gab, like Spouter,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I donן¿½t say he have the practice, BUT I SAY HE HAVE THE PLUCK!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Now the countryן¿½s gone to ruin, and the Governments are rotten,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But heן¿½ll save the public credit and purtect the public tin;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½To the iverlastinן¿½ glory of the name of God-Forgotten
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Vote for Blazes and Protection, and the land yeן¿½re livinן¿½ in!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pat Mן¿½D. went on the war-path, and he worked like salts and senna,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For he organised committees full of energy and push;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And those wild committees riding through the whisky-fed Gehenna
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Routed out astonished voters from their humpies in the bush.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Everything on wheels was ן¿½rinted,ן¿½ and half-sobered drunks were shot in;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said Mן¿½Durmer to the driver, ן¿½If ye want to save yer skin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Never stop to wet yer whistles, drive like hell to God-Forgotten,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Make the villains plump for Blazes, and the land theyן¿½re livin, in.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Half the local long-departed (for the purpose resurrected)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Plumped for Blazes and Protection, and the country where they died;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So he topped the poll by sixty, and when Blazes was elected
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was victory and triumph on the God-Forgotten side.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then the boys got up a banquet, and our chairman, Pat Mן¿½Durmer,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was next day discovered sleeping in the local bakerן¿½s bin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All the dough had risen round him, but we heard a smothered murmur,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Vote for Blazes, and Protection, and the land yeן¿½re livinן¿½ in.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now the great Sir William Blazes lives in London, ן¿½cross the waters
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they say his city mansion is the swellest in West End,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But I very often wonder if his torey sons and daughters
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ever heard of Billy Blazes who was once the ן¿½peopleן¿½s friend.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Does his biassed memory linger round that wild electioneering
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the men of God-Forgotten stuck to him through thick and thin?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Does he ever, in his dreaming, hear the cry above the cheering:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Vote for Blazes and Protection, and the land youן¿½re livinן¿½ in?ן¿½


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ah, the bush was grand in those days, and the Western boys were daisies,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And their scheming and their dodging would outdo the wildest print;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still my recollection lingers round the time when Billy Blazes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was returned by God-Forgotten to the ן¿½Commin Parlymintן¿½:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still I keep a sign of canvas, ן¿½twas a mate of mine that made it,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And its paint is cracked and powdered, and its threads are bare and thin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet upon its grimy surface you can read in letters faded:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Vote for Blazes and Protection, and the land youן¿½re livinן¿½ in.ן¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
